items.q: What is the difference between monetary and fiscal policy?
items.correct: Monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply controlled by the central bank; fiscal policy uses government taxation and spending controlled by the Treasury.
items.misconception: Monetary policy is about money and fiscal policy is about taxes.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same thing — government economic policy.
items.why: While the rough distinction has some truth, the key analytical point is WHO controls each (independent central bank vs elected government) — central to debates on policy independence.

items.q: How does quantitative easing actually work?
items.correct: The central bank creates reserves to buy financial assets (mostly government bonds) from banks and pension funds, raising asset prices and lowering yields — stimulating spending and lending indirectly. It is not literally printing physical cash for the public.
items.misconception: QE is the central bank printing money and handing it to citizens.
items.random_wrong: QE is when banks charge negative interest on deposits.
items.why: QE works through asset markets and bank reserves, not direct cash transfers. Students picture printing presses and helicopter drops, missing the indirect channel via bond yields, exchange rates and wealth effects.

items.correct: Features of the tax and benefit system that automatically cushion the economy without new legislation — e.g. unemployment benefits rise in slumps; tax revenues fall as incomes fall, supporting demand.
items.misconception: Automatic stabilisers are interest rate changes by the central bank.
items.random_wrong: Automatic stabilisers are special government emergency funds.
items.why: Stabilisers are fiscal (tax/benefit) and automatic (no decision needed) — distinct from monetary policy or discretionary stimulus. Students confuse them with central bank action or with one-off bailout funds.

items.q: A nudge is best described as:
items.correct: A small choice-architecture change that steers behaviour
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A small choice-architecture change that steers behaviour. “A legal ban” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Changing the default to opt-in. “Banning opt-out” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Richard Thaler won the Nobel Prize?
items.why: Exact answer: 2017. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
